result.diagram_code:
sequenceDiagram
participant Initiator as Initiator(A)
participant Responder as Responder(B)
participant Server
participant KeyReg as KeyRegistry
participant Attacker
Attacker->>KeyReg: register (B, pk(attacker))
Attacker->>Server: request certificate for B
Server->>Attacker: sign((pk(attacker), B), skS)
Attacker->>Initiator: deliver signed cert for B
Initiator->>Attacker: encrypt((Na, A), pk(attacker))
Attacker->>Attacker: decrypt and learn Na
Attacker->>Responder: encrypt((Na, A), pk(B))
Responder->>Server: request certificate for A
Server->>Responder: sign((pk(A), A), skS)
Responder->>Attacker: encrypt((Na, Nb, B), pk(A))
Attacker->>Initiator: forward encrypt((Na, Nb, B), pk(A))
Initiator->>Attacker: encrypt(Nb, pk(attacker))
Attacker->>Attacker: decrypt and learn Nb
Initiator->>Attacker: sencrypt(secretANa, Na)
Initiator->>Attacker: sencrypt(secretANb, Nb)
result.explanation: The attack comes from the attacker-writable key table. Via processK, the attacker inserts pk(attacker) for an honest identity such as A or B, and processS then signs that bogus binding. Both roles accept the signed fake certificate, so the attacker can substitute keys, recover Na and/or Nb from protocol traffic, decrypt the corresponding sencrypt(secret..., nonce) outputs, and also make sessions terminate without a matching honest peer. This is why all secrecy and correspondence queries fail.
result.patch_suggestion: Do not allow untrusted key registration for honest hosts. Remove or restrict !processK so only trusted setup inserts keys(A, pkA) and keys(B, pkB), or enforce authenticated ownership and reject attacker-supplied inserts/overwrites for A and B. Then processS will certify only the genuine public keys.
result.notes: The traces are driven by forged key-table entries. The model also hard-codes skA/skB in the initiator/responder checks instead of the selected role key skxA/skxB, which is worth reviewing separately.
